title: 2025/10/30 - PP (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-10-29 - Primary Production Committee - Part 2 [Subcommittee A, Part 2]
description: Thursday, 30 October 2025 | Primary Production Subcommittee A Part 2 Animal Welfare (Regulations for Management of Pigs) Amendment Bill - hearing of evidence Nadja Isabell Stubbe Graeme Hansen Lynley Hunter Lichelle Evans Bridget Thompson

Um, So my submission is grounded in over two decades of professional experience across the mental health and criminal justice sectors.

Before I begin, when I speak about farmers or industry, I am not implying that all are abusive. However, after years of public education and scientific evidence, ignorance is no longer an excuse. I strongly oppose this bill. This bill says a great deal about who we are as a nation. It, it is a clear indication that we're willing to turn a blind eye to extreme animal and human suffering. Given the short time I have, I will focus on two key neglected issues. The psychological violence inflicted on the public and the systemic failure of enforcement and judicial fairness.

Harm to animals does not occur in isolation from harm to humans. For many people, witnessing or knowing about the systemic cruelty creates secondary trauma and produces a psychological injury. The industry has no right to burden the public with the psychological cost of their failure to comply.

The cost of this trauma is being transferred onto us. When we speak of family violence, we include physical, sexual, and psychological violence. Psychological violence can cause more harm than physical violence. Our family violence act already defines psychological abuse to include the ill treatment of pets and other animals whose welfare affects a person's wellbeing.

This provision recognizes the suffering of sentient beings and its impact on humans. The cruelty sanctioned by this bill as a national scale perpetration psychological violence. It is clear that animal welfare can detrimentally impact people's mental health.

However, there has been no public response from the Minister of Mental Health slamming this bill. This is deeply concerning. I can say without hesitation that suicidal ideation, self-harm, and substance abuse has increased as a direct result of this bill.
